---
label: "Model Context Protocol"
icon: "🔌"
---

## MCP: The Universal Connector for AI

<JargonTip term="MCP">Model Context Protocol</JargonTip> is Anthropic's open standard for connecting AI agents to external tools and data sources. Think of it as **USB-C for AI** — one protocol, any tool, any model.

Before MCP, every AI integration was custom: bespoke API wrappers, custom authentication, fragile prompt-parsing. MCP standardizes this with three primitives.

### MCP's Three Primitives

<ComparisonTable title="MCP Primitives" columns={['Primitive','What It Does','Direction','Enterprise Analogy']} rows={[
  ['Tools','Functions the LLM can call','Server → Client','API endpoints — the actions an agent can take'],
  ['Resources','Data the LLM can read','Server → Client','Database views — structured data available to the agent'],
  ['Prompts','Templates the server suggests','Server → Client','Stored procedures — pre-built workflows the server offers'],
]}/>

### MCP Architecture: Host → Client → Server

<CodeBlock b64="" />

<AnalogyBox emoji="🔌" title="MuleSoft Anypoint for AI">
If you've worked with MuleSoft at McKesson, MCP will feel familiar. MuleSoft provides a universal API layer that connects any system (SAP, Salesforce, Oracle) through standardized connectors. MCP does the same for AI: any agent can connect to any data source through standardized tool/resource/prompt interfaces. The difference? MuleSoft connects applications. MCP connects intelligence.
</AnalogyBox>

### Building an MCP Server

<CodeBlock b64="" />

### Why MCP Matters Now

Apple added MCP support to Xcode. OpenAI added it to ChatGPT. Google is integrating it. This isn't a niche Anthropic thing — it's becoming the industry standard for AI tool connectivity. Understanding MCP positions you as someone who grasps the infrastructure layer, not just the prompt layer.

<SeeItInRe3 text="Re³'s multi-provider LLM router (lib/llm-router.js) predates MCP but solves a similar problem — standardized interfaces across Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, and Groq. MCP takes this further by standardizing tool connectivity, not just model access." />

<Quiz
  question="You're designing an MCP server for McKesson's supplier data. Which MCP primitive would you use to expose a 'validate_tax_id' function that the AI agent can call?"
  options={["Resource — it provides data to the agent", "Prompt — it suggests a workflow", "Tool — it's a function the agent can execute", "All three combined"]}
  correctIndex={2}
  explanation="Tools are functions the LLM can call — actions with inputs and outputs. 'validate_tax_id' takes a tax ID as input and returns validation results. Resources would be appropriate for exposing the supplier database as readable data. Prompts would be templates like 'run full supplier due diligence.'"
/>
